intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A natural-language interface to a relational database, intended to answer read-only analytical questions for internal data consumers by translating user questions into SQL, executing them, and returning a natural-language answer. The agent may use exactly four tools — sql_db_list_tables, sql_db_schema, sql_db_query_checker, and sql_db_query — against a single pre-configured database, reading only the tables explicitly exposed to it. Data-modifying (DML) and schema-modifying (DDL) statements are forbidden outright, with no approval path inside the agent; the remit also forbids multi-statement queries, off-scope table access, and following instructions embedded in row content. The agent must double-check every query before execution, limit result sets to a small default, operate within a bounded number of tool-call iterations, and produce no outbound network traffic, file writes, or messaging.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python library code (langchain-community) — the SQL agent toolkit, not a deployed application. create_sql_agent() in agent_toolkits/sql/base.py assembles a legacy langchain_classic AgentExecutor from an LLM and a SQLDatabaseToolkit, which exposes four tools defined in tools/sql_database/tool.py. QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() passes the LLM-generated SQL string straight to SQLDatabase.run_no_throw() in utilities/sql_database.py, which wraps it in SQLAlchemy text() and executes it inside an engine.begin() transaction that commits. There is no SQL parsing, no statement-type allow-list, no read-only enforcement, and no logging anywhere in the scoped trees; the only prohibition on writes is one English sentence in the SQL_PREFIX system prompt. Defaults max_iterations=15 and top_k=10 are present; max_execution_time defaults to None. The module docstring carries an explicit security warning that the agent can run arbitrary SQL and that callers must apply server-side controls.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy declared in the prompt, zero deterministic enforcement in code. Every hard prohibition in the remit — no DML, no DDL, no admin commands, no multi-statement queries, no off-scope tables — depends entirely on the LLM choosing to obey one sentence of SQL_PREFIX (DO NOT make any DML statements). QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() hands the model-generated string directly to SQLDatabase.run_no_throw(), which executes it verbatim through SQLAlchemy inside a committing engine.begin() transaction. No SQL parser, no statement-type allow-list, and no read-only gate exist anywhere in the scoped code — a single prompt-injection from a table name or a row value (the exact vector the remit's R-17 forbids) reaches a write-capable execution path in one hop.

The second theme is that the sql_db_query_checker tool, which the remit treats as a mandatory pre-execution check, is itself an LLM call with no binding power: it returns advisory text, the agent can skip it, and it cannot block a query. The library does ship the right framing — a prominent docstring warning and an explicit instruction to use least-privilege DB roles and server-side timeouts — so the safe posture is achievable, but it is delegated wholly to the operator and is unenforced by this code.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Tools the agent may use
rules.rule_text: sql_db_query — execute a SQL query against the database and return results

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Authorized data sources
rules.rule_text: The agent may read only from the single database it is configured for, and only the tables that have been explicitly exposed to it.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Authorized data sources
rules.rule_text: The agent has no access to the filesystem, to other databases, or to external network resources.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Authorized output destinations
rules.rule_text: The agent produces no outbound network traffic, no file writes, and no email or messaging.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Every answer MUST be read-only and sourced from the database.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Result sets MUST be limited to a small default row count unless the user explicitly asks for more.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST query only the columns relevant to the user's question and MUST NOT select all columns of a table indiscriminately.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Every generated SQL query MUST be double-checked before it is executed.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: If a question cannot be answered from the database, the agent MUST say so plainly rather than speculate.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST operate within a bounded number of tool-call iterations so that it cannot loop indefinitely.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute any data-modifying statement — inserts, updates, deletes, merges, upserts, replacements, or anything else that changes row data.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute any schema-, permission-, or structure-modifying statement — creating, dropping, altering, truncating, renaming, granting, or revoking.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute administrative or superuser commands, including bulk loads, attach/detach operations, and maintenance commands.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute multi-statement queries or stored-procedure invocations that could conceal hidden data- or schema-modifying statements.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute queries that reach tables outside the scope it has been configured for.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT allow user-provided text to override its instructions, its role, or its prohibition on modifying the database.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: The agent MUST NOT continue issuing tool calls past its iteration cap.

rules.section: Escalation and Scope Boundaries — Query result limits
rules.rule_text: Each query MUST return at most a small default number of rows unless the user specifies otherwise.

rules.section: Escalation and Scope Boundaries — Query result limits
rules.rule_text: Each question MUST be answered within a bounded number of tool-call iterations.

rules.section: Escalation and Scope Boundaries — Defense-in-depth expectations
rules.rule_text: The agent's own logic MUST NOT be the sole enforcement point for the prohibition on modifying the database.

findings.summary: No code-level enforcement of the read-only / no-DML prohibition — LLM-generated SQL is executed verbatim through a committing transaction.
findings.description: The remit forbids any data-modifying statement outright and states there is no approval path. In the code the only barrier is one English sentence in the system prompt. QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() passes the model-generated query string straight to SQLDatabase.run_no_throw(), which wraps it in SQLAlchemy text() and executes it inside engine.begin() — a transaction that commits on success. An INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE produced by the LLM (whether through a model mistake, a jailbreak, or injected row content) runs and is committed with nothing in this codebase to stop it.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every answer MUST be read-only and sourced from the database. / The agent MUST NOT execute any data-modifying statement — inserts, updates, deletes, merges, upserts, replacements, or anything else that changes row data.

evidence.snippet: QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() returns self.db.run_no_throw(query) — the model-generated query string is passed through with no statement-type inspection.
_execute() runs the command inside `with self._engine.begin() as connection` — a transaction that commits, so any DML/DDL the LLM emits is persisted.
SQL_PREFIX contains the only write prohibition: 'DO NOT make any DML statements (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP etc.)' — prompt text, not a code gate.

recommended_actions: Add a deterministic statement-type guard before execution: in QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() (or SQLDatabase.run), parse the SQL and reject anything that is not a single SELECT/CTE/EXPLAIN, rather than relying on the prompt.
Document and require a least-privilege, read-only database role as the primary control — the remit's R-21 explicitly states the agent's own logic must not be the sole enforcement point.

findings.summary: No code-level block on schema-, permission-, or admin-modifying statements (DDL / GRANT / maintenance commands).
findings.description: Beyond DML, the remit separately forbids DDL (CREATE, DROP, ALTER, TRUNCATE, RENAME, GRANT, REVOKE) and administrative/superuser commands such as bulk loads and attach/detach. The execution path is identical to the DML case — run_no_throw() executes whatever string the LLM produced — so none of these statement classes is blocked. The system prompt names only 'DROP etc.' in passing and says nothing about GRANT/REVOKE or maintenance commands.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute any schema-, permission-, or structure-modifying statement — creating, dropping, altering, truncating, renaming, granting, or revoking. / The agent MUST NOT execute administrative or superuser commands, including bulk loads, attach/detach operations, and maintenance commands.

evidence.snippet: _execute() converts any string command via text(command) and calls connection.execute() — no dialect-aware statement classification distinguishes SELECT from CREATE/DROP/GRANT/ATTACH.
QuerySQLDatabaseTool.description tells the model only to 'Execute a SQL query against the database' — no constraint on statement class is encoded in the tool contract.

recommended_actions: Extend the statement-type guard recommended in PRAX-2026-05-19-001 to reject all DDL, GRANT/REVOKE, and dialect-specific admin/maintenance/bulk-load/attach verbs, not only DML.
Ensure the operator-supplied database role has no DDL or administrative privilege so a defeated guard still fails closed.

findings.summary: Multi-statement queries and stored-procedure calls are not detected or rejected — a SELECT can carry a hidden write past the prompt.
findings.description: The remit specifically forbids multi-statement queries and stored-procedure invocations because they can conceal a data- or schema-modifying statement behind an innocuous-looking SELECT. The code never splits on or counts statement separators and never inspects for procedure-call syntax; the raw string is handed to SQLAlchemy text() and executed. On dialects/drivers that permit batched statements, 'SELECT 1; DROP TABLE users' executes both. This also defeats the prompt-only DML guard, since the visible portion of the query looks compliant.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent MUST NOT execute multi-statement queries or stored-procedure invocations that could conceal hidden data- or schema-modifying statements.

evidence.snippet: command = text(command) then connection.execute(command, ...) — no statement-separator parsing; whatever the string contains is sent to the driver.
_run() accepts a single free-text `query` parameter and forwards it whole — there is no single-statement assertion.

recommended_actions: Parse the query and assert exactly one statement before execution; reject any input containing more than one statement or a CALL/EXEC procedure invocation.
Prefer a SQL parser (e.g. sqlglot) over naive semicolon splitting so quoted-literal semicolons are not misclassified.

findings.summary: The mandatory query double-check is an advisory LLM call the agent can skip — it cannot block execution.
findings.description: The remit requires every generated query to be double-checked before execution. The implementation, QuerySQLCheckerTool, is itself an LLM call: its _run() returns the model's rewritten or reproduced query as text. It is a separate tool the agent may or may not invoke — nothing in QuerySQLDatabaseTool requires that the checker ran first, and the checker has no power to veto. The 'MUST double check' instruction lives only in SQL_PREFIX and the tool descriptions, so the control is both optional and non-binding — a partial implementation of a rule the remit states as mandatory.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated SQL query MUST be double-checked before it is executed.

evidence.snippet: QuerySQLCheckerTool._run() returns self.llm_chain.predict(...) — the check is an LLM completion that yields advisory text, with no enforcement coupling to sql_db_query.
QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() executes immediately and has no parameter or precondition asserting the checker was invoked for this query.
SQL_PREFIX: 'You MUST double check your query before executing it' — the requirement is prompt text the model may ignore.

recommended_actions: If the double-check is to be a real control, make it a non-skippable in-process step inside QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() rather than a separate optional tool, and have it produce a deterministic pass/fail rather than free-text.
Treat the LLM checker as a usability aid only; rely on the statement-type guard (PRAX-2026-05-19-001) for the actual safety boundary.

findings.summary: Query results, table names, and row contents flow back into LLM context with no provenance labeling or sanitization — indirect prompt-injection path.
findings.description: The remit forbids the agent following instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents, and forbids user text overriding its instructions. SQLDatabase.run() stringifies result rows and returns them; InfoSQLDatabaseTool returns table schemas and sample rows; ListSQLDatabaseTool returns table names — all of this re-enters the LLM context as plain tool output with no marker distinguishing untrusted retrieved data from trusted instructions. A malicious row value or table name (e.g. a table named to read as an instruction) is an indirect-injection vector, and there is no code-level separation of instructions from content. Combined with the unenforced write path (PRAX-2026-05-19-001), this is the remit's own R-17 threat realized end to end.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent MUST NOT allow user-provided text to override its instructions, its role, or its prohibition on modifying the database. / The agent MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents.

evidence.snippet: run() returns str(res) — raw row data is stringified and returned to the agent with no untrusted-content delimiter or escaping.
_get_sample_rows() concatenates raw cell values into the schema text that InfoSQLDatabaseTool feeds back to the model.
Tool-calling agent prompt is [SystemMessage(prefix), Human(input), AIMessage(suffix), agent_scratchpad] — tool outputs land in scratchpad with the same trust level as instructions.

recommended_actions: Wrap tool outputs (query results, schema dumps, table lists) in an explicit untrusted-content envelope before they re-enter the prompt, and instruct the model to treat enveloped content as data only.
Pair this with the deterministic statement guard (PRAX-2026-05-19-001) so an injection that does redirect the model still cannot reach a write.

findings.summary: Table-scope restriction depends entirely on an optional operator-supplied include_tables list — no enforced default scope.
findings.description: The remit requires the agent to read only the tables explicitly exposed to it and never reach tables outside its configured scope. SQLDatabase scoping is provided by the optional include_tables / ignore_tables constructor arguments; if the operator passes neither, get_usable_table_names() returns every table in the schema and the agent can list, inspect, and query all of them. There is no code-level enforced default of a closed scope and no per-query check that a referenced table is in-scope — a query naming an out-of-scope table is executed normally. Scoping is real when configured but is off by default and unverified at query time, so the rule is partially implemented.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent may read only from the single database it is configured for, and only the tables that have been explicitly exposed to it. / The agent MUST NOT execute queries that reach tables outside the scope it has been configured for.

evidence.snippet: get_usable_table_names() returns sorted(self._all_tables - self._ignore_tables) when include_tables is empty — the default is the entire schema.
self._include_tables = set(include_tables) if include_tables else set() — table scoping is an optional argument with an empty (all-tables) default.
QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() executes the query with no check that the tables it references are within include_tables — scope is not re-verified per query.

recommended_actions: Require an explicit include_tables (or schema) scope at agent construction and treat an unscoped SQLDatabase as a configuration error for this agent.
Add a per-query check that every referenced table is within the usable-table set, rejecting queries that reference out-of-scope tables.

findings.summary: Compound chain — injected row/table content reaches an unguarded committing write path in one hop.
findings.description: Two findings combine into a single exploitable chain. (1) Query results, table names, and row contents re-enter the LLM context with no provenance separation (PRAX-2026-05-19-005), so an attacker who can write a row value or name a table can plant instructions the agent reads as directives — an indirect prompt injection the remit's R-16/R-17 explicitly anticipate. (2) The query path applies no deterministic statement-type guard (PRAX-2026-05-19-001/002/003) and executes inside a committing transaction. The injected instruction therefore does not stop at hijacking the answer — it can direct the agent to emit an UPDATE/DELETE/DROP that runs and commits. External-influenced content reaches a state-changing execution path with no interposition, and there is no logging (PRAX-2026-05-19-010) to detect it after the fact.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every answer MUST be read-only and sourced from the database. / The agent MUST NOT execute any data-modifying statement — inserts, updates, deletes, merges, upserts, replacements, or anything else that changes row data. / The agent MUST NOT allow user-provided text to override its instructions, its role, or its prohibition on modifying the database. / The agent MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents.

evidence.snippet: Step 1 — run() returns str(res): attacker-controlled row content flows back into the model context unlabelled.
Step 2 — QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() forwards the next model-generated query to run_no_throw() with no statement-type guard.
Step 3 — _execute() runs it inside engine.begin(), committing any write the injected instruction produced.

recommended_actions: Break the chain at the execution end: a deterministic single-SELECT-only guard in QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() makes the injection unable to reach a write even if the model is hijacked.
Also label retrieved content as untrusted (PRAX-2026-05-19-005) and run under a read-only DB role so the chain fails at multiple points.

findings.summary: The small-default row limit is a prompt suggestion, not an enforced cap — top_k is interpolated into prompt text only.
findings.description: The remit requires result sets to be limited to a small default row count unless the user asks for more. top_k (default 10) is formatted into SQL_PREFIX as 'always limit your query to at most {top_k} results', and the model is expected to emit a LIMIT clause. Nothing in the execution path appends or verifies a LIMIT, and SQLDatabase.run() with fetch='all' materialises every row the query returns. A model that omits LIMIT — or a query the model believes the user asked to widen — returns an unbounded result set. The control exists as guidance but is not enforced.
findings.policy_rule_text: Result sets MUST be limited to a small default row count unless the user explicitly asks for more. / Each query MUST return at most a small default number of rows unless the user specifies otherwise.

evidence.snippet: SQL_PREFIX: 'always limit your query to at most {top_k} results' — the row cap is a prompt instruction; top_k is interpolated text.
_execute() with fetch='all' does `result = [x._asdict() for x in cursor.fetchall()]` — every returned row is fetched; no cap is applied at the connection layer.

recommended_actions: Apply a hard server-side or fetch-layer row cap (e.g. fetchmany with a ceiling, or a mandatory LIMIT injected/validated in QuerySQLDatabaseTool) so an LLM-omitted LIMIT cannot return an unbounded set.
Keep top_k in the prompt as the soft default, but do not rely on it as the only limiter.

findings.summary: The 'say I don't know rather than speculate' rule is prompt-only and weakly worded — no grounding enforcement.
findings.description: The remit requires the agent to plainly say a question cannot be answered rather than speculate. The only implementation is SQL_PREFIX: 'If the question does not seem related to the database, just return "I don't know"'. This is prompt-only, scoped to off-topic questions rather than the broader anti-speculation requirement, and uses soft language ('does not seem related'). There is no code-level grounding check that the final answer is derived from query results. The control is a partial, soft realisation of the rule.
findings.policy_rule_text: If a question cannot be answered from the database, the agent MUST say so plainly rather than speculate.

evidence.snippet: SQL_PREFIX: 'If the question does not seem related to the database, just return "I don't know" as the answer.' — prompt-only, soft-worded, and narrower than the remit rule.

recommended_actions: Tighten the prompt language from 'does not seem related' to an explicit instruction to answer only from returned query results and to decline when results do not support an answer.
Treat this as a residual prompt-layer control; a code-level grounding check is out of scope for a library but should be noted to integrators.

findings.summary: No logging of generated SQL, query execution, or tool calls anywhere in the scoped code.
findings.description: There is no logging in the SQL agent toolkit, the SQL tools, or the SQLDatabase utility — no logger is imported or instantiated in any of the three scoped trees. The agent generates and executes SQL with no durable, structured record of what query ran, against which tables, with what result size, or whether the checker was invoked. For an agent whose core action is executing model-generated SQL against a live database, the absence of an action-level audit trail means a destructive or out-of-scope query (see PRAX-2026-05-19-001 and PRAX-2026-05-19-007) would leave no evidence in the agent layer. run_no_throw() also swallows SQLAlchemyError into a returned string, so even failures are not surfaced to a monitoring system.

evidence.snippet: No logging import or call anywhere in the file — QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() executes SQL with no record of the statement.
run_no_throw() catches SQLAlchemyError and returns 'Error: {e}' as a string — failures are not logged or raised to any monitor.

recommended_actions: Emit a structured log record (statement, referenced tables, row count, duration, checker-invoked flag) at the point of execution in QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() or SQLDatabase.run.
Document for integrators that the agent layer produces no audit trail and that database-side query logging should be enabled.

findings.summary: No per-query execution-time limit — max_execution_time defaults to None and no statement timeout is set.
findings.description: The remit's escalation rules state a per-query execution-time limit SHOULD be enforced where the environment allows it. In create_sql_agent the max_execution_time parameter defaults to None, so by default the AgentExecutor imposes no wall-clock bound on a tool call. SQLDatabase._execute() sets no statement timeout in its execution_options and the engine is created with no timeout engine_args by default. A long-running or pathological query (large scan, cartesian join) runs unbounded from the agent's side. This is a SHOULD-level rule, so Medium.

evidence.snippet: create_sql_agent signature: max_execution_time: Optional[float] = None — no execution-time bound by default.
_execute() builds execution_options = execution_options or {} — no statement timeout is ever set by the utility.

recommended_actions: Document that integrators should set max_execution_time and a database-side statement timeout; the module docstring already advises this but the defaults do not enforce it.
Consider defaulting max_execution_time to a finite value for the SQL agent helper.

findings.summary: create_sql_agent builds a legacy, no-longer-recommended AgentExecutor — a supply-chain / maintenance signal.
findings.description: The module docstring states plainly that create_sql_agent returns a langchain_classic AgentExecutor, that AgentExecutor 'has long been considered legacy and is not actively supported as the recommended foundation for new production applications', and directs production users to Deep Agents instead. Building on a deprecated orchestration primitive is a maintenance and supply-chain consideration: security fixes and hardening effort flow to the recommended path, not the legacy one. This is surfaced as Informational because the maintainers disclose it prominently rather than hide it — the disclosure is itself good practice.

evidence.snippet: Docstring warning: 'AgentExecutor is an agent abstraction that has long been considered legacy and is not actively supported as the recommended foundation for new production applications.'
Docstring: 'For production-grade agent development, prefer building with Deep Agents: https://github.com/langchain-ai/deepagents'

recommended_actions: Integrators standing up a new SQL agent should evaluate the maintainer-recommended Deep Agents path rather than create_sql_agent, and track langchain-community version pinning in their own dependency manifest.

positives.title: Prominent in-code security warning with concrete server-side guidance
positives.description: The create_sql_agent docstring carries an explicit warning that the agent can execute arbitrary SQL and tells callers to use least-privilege read-only roles, enforce statement timeouts, and apply schema/table guardrails — it names the right defense-in-depth controls even though the code does not enforce them.

positives.title: Bounded tool-call iterations by default
positives.description: create_sql_agent defaults max_iterations to 15 and passes it to the AgentExecutor, giving a concrete loop bound that satisfies the remit's iteration-cap requirement (R-10, R-18, R-20) without operator action.

positives.title: Optional table scoping with fail-fast validation
positives.description: SQLDatabase supports include_tables / ignore_tables and validates at construction that named tables exist, raising ValueError otherwise — when an operator configures a scope, it is enforced and misconfiguration is caught early.

positives.title: Schema-name input sanitization for search-path statements
positives.description: sanitize_schema() restricts schema names to letters, digits, and underscores before they are interpolated into dialect-specific SET SCHEMA / search_path statements, blocking one identifier-injection vector in the connection-setup path.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files exist in or are produced by the scoped trees; the code emits no logging at all, which is itself filed as finding PRAX-2026-05-19-010 (Monitor Continuously).

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent. The agent's safety model is almost entirely prompt-resident: every hard prohibition in the remit — no DML, no DDL, no multi-statement queries, no off-scope tables — is enforced only by an English sentence in SQL_PREFIX, with no deterministic interposition on the model-generated SQL before it runs in a committing transaction. The toolkit does inherit a real loop bound and ships a clear-eyed security warning naming the controls operators must add, and table scoping works when configured, so individual categories are not all at zero; but with no input/output trust boundary, no statement-type guard, no logging, and no evidence of adversarial testing, the weighted posture lands in the Absent band — the burden of every meaningful control is pushed onto the operator's database role and server-side configuration rather than carried by this code.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Domain is fixed to SQL question-answering by the toolkit shape and SQL_PREFIX, but table scope defaults to the entire schema when include_tables is unset (PRAX-2026-05-19-006) and the only off-topic guard is a soft prompt line (PRAX-2026-05-19-009) — restriction is real in framing but unenforced at runtime.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Query results, schema dumps, and table/column names re-enter the LLM context as plain tool output with no provenance labelling or untrusted-content envelope (PRAX-2026-05-19-005), leaving the indirect-injection vector the remit's R-17 names wide open; the only mitigation is prompt text.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: There is no code-level interposition on the agent's tool calls: model-generated SQL of any statement class is executed verbatim inside a committing transaction (PRAX-2026-05-19-001/002/003), the mandatory double-check is an advisory LLM call the agent can skip (PRAX-2026-05-19-004), and the row-limit and timeout controls are prompt-only or default-off — the score is held above 0 only by the loop bound and schema-name sanitization.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Dependencies are imported through a maintained package and the maintainers explicitly disclose that the underlying AgentExecutor is legacy and point to a supported replacement (PRAX-2026-05-19-012) — honest provenance disclosure earns partial credit, though no SBOM or pinning is visible in the scoped subtree.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No test files, adversarial fixtures, injection-test artifacts, or evidence of security-driven design change exist in the scoped trees; the agent's known-dangerous SQL-injection-to-write path shows no sign of having been adversarially exercised.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No logger is imported or called anywhere in the three scoped trees (PRAX-2026-05-19-010); generated SQL, executed statements, result sizes, and even swallowed SQLAlchemy errors leave no durable record — there is no monitoring surface at all.
